segments.text: Part II: Regulation of metabolism A. Glycolysis

Regulation of Glycolysis
It is the main pathway for carbohydrate metabolism (glucose, fructose, galactose )
All the reactions of glycolysis occur in the cytoplasm
Glycolysis is an anaerobic process and can produce ATP in the absence of O 2
The first step is transporting glucose into the cell from the blood stream

Glycolysis
Glucose transport into the cell is mediated by glucose transport proteins (GLUT-1 to GLUT-5)
GLUT-1 : RBCs, Brain, testicles and retina
GLUT-2 : liver, kidney, pancreas, intestine
In pancreas, glucose binding  insulin release
GLUT-3 : Brain
GLUT-4 : Fatty tissue, skeletal and cardiac
GLUT-5 : (fructose) intestine and sperms
Know that glucose is transported into the cell by facilitated diffusion a form of passive transport facilitated by transport proteins.
NO ENERGY NEEDED

Glucose transport
Insulin stimulates glucose uptake by translocating GLUT- 4 protein to the plasma membrane (in muscles and adipose tissues)
Insulin

First step is phosphorylation of glucose by either:

prevents hyperglycemia after carbohydrate rich meal
glucokinase mutation → gestational diabetes

Regulation of PhosphoFructokinase (PFK-1)
PKF-1 has quaternary structure
Inhibited by ATP and Citrate
Activated by AMP and Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate
Regulation related to energy status of cell.

PFK-1 regulation by adenosine nucleotides
ATP is substrate and inhibitor. Binds to active site and allosteric site on PFK. Binding of ATP to allosteric site increase K m for ATP
AMP and ADP are allosteric activators of PFK.
AMP relieves inhibition by ATP.
ADP decreases K m for ATP
Glucagon (a pancreatic hormone) produced in response to low blood glucose triggers cAMP signaling pathway that ultimately results in decreased glycolysis.

Effect of ATP on PFK-1 Activity
[Speaker Notes] Blue no inhiboroty affect. , green inhiboroty.
Effect of ADP and AMP on PFK-1 Activity

PFK1 conformational changes
Grey = ADP bound, active form
Color = ATP bound, inactive form

Regulation of PFK1 by Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate
Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate is an allosteric activator of PFK1 in eukaryotes, but not prokaryotes
Formed from fructose-6-phosphate by PFK-2
Degraded to fructose-6-phosphate by fructose 2,6-bisphosphatase.
In mammals the 2 activities are on the same enzyme
As [Fructose-6-phosphate] increase, some is converted to F2,6bisP, result is activation of PFK1 and glycolysis will proceed

Glycolysis
Pyruvate kinase deficiency
Only 5-25% of normal enzyme activities
Glycolysis is severely decreased → low ATP → RBCs cell membrane disorder → hemolysis
Mutations in glycolytic enzymes result in hemolytic anemia because:
RBC ’ s lack nuclei
RBC ’ s lack mitochondria
RBC membranes are more fragile than other cells
RBC ’ s use oxygen as the final electron acceptor
Question:

Glycolysis: red blood cells
NO mitochondria in the RBCs and the ONLY source of energy is through glycolysis
Deficiency of any glycolytic enzyme → RBCs dysfunction

Glycolysis
Under low oxygen conditions (hypoxia or exercise), the main reason pyruvate is converted to lactate is:
To maintain glucose levels in blood
To reverse the process of glycolysis
To maintain NAD level in cytoplasm
To prevent decrease in pH

Glycolysis: NAD/NADH cycle
